commonIssues:
Applicants struggle to provide pre-independence or pre-constitutional documents (e.g., prior to 1950 for SC, 1961 for VJ/NT, 1967 for OBC/SBC) to prove residence, which is a mandatory requirement.

How to fix:

  • Obtain old land records, property documents, or school records from the IGR Maharashtra or local Talathi/Revenue Office.
  • Request certified copies of village records, school admission registers, or census records from the District Archives or Collector's Office.
  • Submit an affidavit from a gazetted officer or elected representative confirming your community status if original documents are unavailable.
  • If documents are untraceable, approach the Aaple Sarkar Portal and mention the difficulty — the Tahsildar may accept alternate proof.

Many applicants, particularly in rural areas, find it difficult to locate old school leaving certificates or birth records of grandparents to establish lineage.

How to fix:

  • Visit the local Zilla Parishad school or Talathi office to obtain certified copies of old admission registers or birth records.
  • Request a 7/12 extract (Satbara Utara) from the Mahabhulekh Portal — old land records often mention caste.
  • Submit a sworn affidavit from two elders of the same community along with their own caste certificates.
  • Approach the District Caste Scrutiny Committee at the Collectorate for guidance on alternative documentation.

Applications are frequently stalled due to missing documents like affidavits, income certificates, or valid affidavits of relatives.

How to fix:

  • Check the complete document checklist on the Aaple Sarkar Portal before applying.
  • Get affidavits notarized at the local court or through an authorized notary.
  • Obtain income certificates from the Tahsildar office — apply online via Aaple Sarkar under Revenue Department services.
  • Ensure all documents are self-attested and carry original copies for physical verification.

Users have reported issues with the Aaple Sarkar online portal, such as slow performance, technical errors while uploading documents, and difficulties creating a user profile.

How to fix:

  • Use Google Chrome or Mozilla Firefox (latest version) and clear browser cache before accessing the portal.
  • Upload documents in PDF format under 2MB — compress using free tools like SmallPDF.
  • Try during off-peak hours (early morning or late evening) to avoid server load.
  • If profile creation fails, visit the nearest Setu Suvidha Kendra or Common Service Centre (CSC) for assisted registration.
  • Report technical issues via the Maharashtra Grievance Portal.

Applicants often do not know which documents are required for their specific category (SC/ST/OBC/VJ-NT/SBC), leading to rejections.

How to fix:

  • Check the category-specific document checklist on Aaple Sarkar before starting your application.
  • Visit the local Tahsildar office or District Social Welfare Office for guidance on requirements specific to your caste category.
  • Call the Maharashtra Government Helpline 1800-120-8040 (toll-free) for assistance.
  • Consult the Social Justice & Special Assistance Department website for detailed category-wise guidelines.

Despite the Right to Service Act (RTS), there are major delays in receiving certificates, often because of poor manpower at the district committee level.

How to fix:

  • Track your application status on the Aaple Sarkar Portal using your application number.
  • If the timeline under the Right to Service (RTS) Act has been exceeded, file a complaint at the Maharashtra Grievance Portal.
  • Approach the Appellate Authority (SDM/ADM) at the district level for escalation under RTS.
  • Write to the District Collector or contact the 1800-120-8040 helpline.

Many applicants report that their applications are rejected or sent back for clarification by the Tahsildar's office without clear justification.

How to fix:

  • Request a written reason for rejection from the Tahsildar — this is your right under RTS Act.
  • File an appeal with the Sub-Divisional Magistrate (SDM) within 30 days of rejection.
  • Lodge a grievance at the Maharashtra Grievance Portal.
  • If repeatedly rejected, seek legal aid from the District Legal Services Authority (DLSA) at NALSA Portal.

Instances have been reported where a physically held caste certificate shows as 'INVALID' when checked in the online system.

How to fix:

  • Visit the Tahsildar office that issued the original certificate and request re-digitization of your record.
  • Carry the original physical certificate along with the receipt for verification and record correction.
  • File a complaint on Aaple Sarkar under the 'Certificate Verification' section.
  • If unresolved, escalate to the District Collector or the Maharashtra Grievance Portal.

Obtaining the Validity Certificate is far more difficult than the Caste Certificate itself. The scarcity of officials to review applications leads to backlog and delays, putting educational admissions at risk.

How to fix:

  • Apply for the Caste Validity Certificate well in advance (ideally in Class 10) via the Tribal Research & Training Institute (TRTI) for ST or via the Scrutiny Committee at the Collectorate for SC/OBC.
  • Track status through the Aaple Sarkar Portal.
  • If delayed beyond the prescribed timeline, file an appeal with the District Caste Scrutiny Committee Chairman.
  • For educational admission urgency, approach the college/university with a copy of your pending validity application as interim proof.

Sometimes applicants do not receive SMS updates, leaving them unaware of the status of their application.

How to fix:

  • Log in to the Aaple Sarkar Portal and check your application status directly under 'Track Application'.
  • Ensure your registered mobile number is correct in the application form — update it at the Tahsildar office if needed.
  • Call the Aaple Sarkar Helpline 1800-120-8040 (toll-free) for status updates.
  • Visit the local Tahsildar office in person with your application receipt for a physical status check.
